Title: How to Add File Extensions in Windows XP Introduction: File extensions are critical components of files in Windows XP as they help determine which program should be used to open them. By default, some file types may not display their extensions, making it difficult to identify their format or associate them with appropriate software. This tutorial will guide you through adding file extensions in Windows XP using its built-in settings and tools. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Be cautious while changing file extensions; incorrect ones might prevent the file from opening correctly. * Before proceeding, ensure that your system has administrator privileges. * Always back up important data before modifying any system setting. Step 1: Access Folder Options Click 'Start' > 'Control Panel'. If Control Panel is displayed in Category view, click 'Switch to Classic View' located at the left sidebar. Double-click 'Folder Options'. This action opens the Folder Options dialog box where you can customize various aspects related to folders, such as viewing hidden files or altering file name displays including extensions. Step 2: Show All File Extensions In the Folder Options window, select the 'View' tab. Scroll down until you find the option labeled 'Hide file extensions for known file types', then uncheck this box if checked. Click 'Apply' followed by 'OK'. Unchecking 'Hide file extensions for known file types' ensures all file extensions become visible, enabling users to add missing ones manually. Note that checking this box hides most common file extensions but leaves others like .txt visible. Unchecking it makes every extension visible regardless of type. Step 3: Locate The Target File Navigate to the location of the file whose extension needs to be added. You can do this via 'My Computer', 'Documents', 'Downloads', etc., depending on where the file resides. Step 4: Rename The File To Add Its Extension Right-click the target file, choose 'Rename' from the context menu. Type the new file extension immediately preceded by a dot (.) without leaving spaces between words within the filename. For example, change "mydocument" to "mydocument.docx". Press Enter when done. Adding the correct file extension lets Windows recognize what kind of file it is dealing with, thus associating it with the right application during double-clicks. Step 5: Confirm Changes A confirmation prompt appears asking whether you want to change the file extension, potentially affecting the way programs treat the file. Select 'Yes'. Choosing yes confirms the renaming process, thereby assigning the newly typed extension to the file. Step 6: Verify The Newly Assigned Extension To confirm successful assignment, repeat Step 3 to locate the edited file. Check if the new extension appears after the period following the filename. If the new file extension shows up correctly, congratulations! Your actions have been successful. However, if issues persist, consider restarting your computer to let changes take full effect. Conclusion: By carefully following these steps, you now know how to add file extensions in Windows XP effectively. Remember always to exercise caution when editing system settings, especially those concerning files and their associated applications. Over the last 20 years, many new anchor designs have appeared. These anchors are usually designed for small vessels, and are usually not for large ships. See modern designs. And he always seems to anticipate that. If you have asthma, you know how it feels to have a "flare-up." It's hard to breathe. You may cough a lot, or hear a whistling sound in your chest (called wheezing). Your chest may feel tight. You may feel tired and not want to play. Why does this happen? Use a paper horn to see how your lungs work. First, blow into the horn. Air goes in and out. That's what healthy lungs are like. Now squeeze the middle of the horn (like the doctor in the picture). Air can't get in and out. That's like your lungs when you have an asthma flare-up. Of course, lungs aren't exactly like a paper horn. Inside the lungs, air goes in and out through very small tubes. These tubes are called airways. Asthma makes airways a little bit inflamed all the time. (That means swollen and red, like your nose when you have a cold.) Air can still go in and out. You may not notice a problem. But lots of things can bother inflamed airways. Then they get even more swollen. Pushing the air in and out gets harder. Less air gets into your lungs. That's a flare-up. Catching Rayquaza and Deoxys in Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire Introduction: Welcome to our comprehensive guide on catching two of the most elusive and powerful Pok mon in Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire - Rayquaza and Deoxys! These legendary creatures are highly sought after by trainers due to their incredible stats and unique abilities. However, capturing them is no easy feat. This tutorial will provide you with an in-depth explanation of every step required to add these mythical beasts to your collection. So let's get started! Step 1: Progress Through The Main Storyline Before attempting to catch either Rayquaza or Deoxys, ensure that you have progressed far enough into the main storyline. For Rayquaza, complete up until just before entering the Sky Pillar (after defeating Team Aqua/Magma at Mt. Chimney). As for Deoxys, finish the Delta Episode which becomes available post-game. Completing these parts of the game unlocks access to both legendaries. Key Tip: Make sure your team is well prepared with strong moves and items like Ultra Balls, Timer Balls, and Healing Items. Also consider having some status-inducing moves such as Sleep Powder or Stun Spore. Step 2: Reach Sky Pillar & Battle Rayquaza Fly to Route 131 using your Eon Flute, then head north towards the Sky Pillar entrance. Once inside, ascend through the tower fighting off Team Magma/Aqua grunts along the way until reaching the summit where Rayquaza resides. Engage in battle with the sky-high serpent. Remember, it's level 70 here so come prepared! Key Guideline: Use Flying-type attacks if possible since they deal double damage to Dragon-types. Additionally, try weakening Rayquaza rather than knocking it out; this makes capture easier later on. Step 3: Weaken And Catch Rayquaza After reducing Rayquaza's health significantly, throw Premier Balls or better yet, Dusk Balls during nighttime encounters, or Timer Balls if more than 10 turns have passed since initiating the battle. Keep throwing balls while avoiding being knocked unconscious yourself. Patience is crucial here. Step 4: Complete The Delta Episode & Unlock Deoxys Post-game, speak to Professor Birch in Littleroot Town to trigger the Delta Episode. Follow the subsequent events leading to battling Deoxys aboard the Space Station. Similar to Rayquaza, defeat but don't kill Deoxys. Key Tip: Unlike Rayquaza, Deoxys changes formes based on its HP percentage. Normal Forme has balanced stats, Speed Forme focuses on speed, Attack Forme boosts attack, and Defense Forme enhances defense. Adjust your strategy accordingly depending on which forme appears. Step 5: Capture Deoxys Like before, aim to lower Deoxys' health considerably without knocking it out. Then toss Ultra Balls, Premier Balls, or repeat balls like Net Ball or Quick Ball, depending on what type advantage you can muster. Wait for the shaking animation indicating successful capture. Conclusion: Capturing Rayquaza and Deoxys requires perseverance and careful planning. With this detailed guide, you now possess all the necessary knowledge to successfully catch these rare Pok mon in Omega Ruby and Alpha Sapphire. Happy hunting! How to Calculate Power Based on Force and Speed In physics, you can calculate power based on force and speed. Because work equals force times distance, you can write the equation for power the following way, assuming that the force acts along the direction of travel: where s is the distance traveled. However, the object's speed, v, is just s divided by t, so the equation breaks down to That's an interesting result - power equals force times speed? Yep, that's what it says. However, because you often have to account for acceleration when you apply a force, you usually write the equation in terms of average power and average speed: Here's an example. Tau is a protein that is found mostly in the neurons of the central nervous system. They help hold together the "microtubules" within the neurons. and when changes happen in the way the tau proteins are supposed to work the microtubules break apart. The tau proteins which are no longer holding the microtubules together form strands called "fibrils", which then clump together inside the neuron to make what are called neurofibrillary tangles . These clumps, also known as 'tau tangles', are all that remain after a neuron has died. In 1848, Karl H. Marx and Friedrich Engels wrote "The Communist Manifesto". [1w Elizabeth tries to grab the doll away from Nina] The area of circles is derived by dividing a circle into an infinite number of wedges formed by radii drawn from the center. When these wedges are rearranged, they form a rectangle whose height is the radius of the circle and whose base length is half of the circumference of the circle. The area of circles are also used in sectors, segments and annuluses. If we want to find the area bound by a circle, well we're going to have to chop this up into pieces of something that we know. And we know the area of a parallelogram is equal to its base times its corresponding height. So what I'm going to do is I'm going to imagine that I can cut this circle up into an infinite number of pieces. But since I can't draw an infinite number of pieces, I'm just going to start by drawing in let's say about 16 different pieces here. So if I could cut out all these pieces and rearrange them, what I would be able to do is I could, let's say we just looked at one of these pieces. So you're going to have the little curvature on the outside form the circle and that both of these are going to be a radius. So what I'm going to do is I'm going to set all of these up in opposing order and if I just continue doing this, I would be able to from a parallelogram where each of these is going to be one of the wedges that I cut out. So I said that my height here is going to be r and I need to find out what is my corresponding base. Well, if I look at this, this outside is part of my circle and this outside right here is also the other part of the circle. So if the whole circumference is equal to 2 pi r, then one of these bases is going to be half of this. And, if I divide this by 2, then half of my circumference is just pi times r. So this distance right here is pi times my radius. So if I know my height is r, and I know my base is pi times r, then I can say the area is just r times r which is r squared times pi. So if I could cut this up into a infinite testable amount of small wedges, I could create a rectangle where I know that my height is r and one of my bases is pi times r and these areas would be equal. Before going further, perhaps it might be well to say a few words of explanation as to the motifs which form the key-notes of Wagner's great musical dramas. When he set his poem of the Nibelungen Ring to music, he was not satisfied with merely beautiful airs and harmonies linked together with no purpose save the lovely sounds. He wished, above all, to have his music fit his words; and for every character and thought and incident, and indeed for almost everything in his operas, he wrote a melody, and these descriptive musical phrases are called _motifs_. Each one has its meaning, and when it is played it brings the thought of what it describes and represents, and it makes a double language-what the characters on the stage are saying and what the music is saying, as well. The Difference between And and Or Date: 01/23/2008 at 11:59:54 From: Mikki Subject: and/or My son had a question that was marked wrong on his paper. He pointed out to me that by the way it was worded, he felt as though he were correct. Here is the question: There are 3 knives, 4 spoons, 4 forks. What fraction of the utensils are spoons OR forks? He answered 4/11 and was told the teachers edition says 8/11. I understand the way he read it to be OR meaning one or the other. If it's 8/11, shouldn't it be worded spoons AND forks? If the answer is 8/11, I want my son to understand why. Date: 01/23/2008 at 12:24:17 From: Doctor Peterson Subject: Re: and/or Hi, Mikki. The words "and" and "or" can be ambiguous in English, so in math we give them precise meanings. We have to teach those meanings, but often forget to, which may have happened here. When we talk about the set of things that are A AND B, we mean that EACH of those things must be BOTH A and B. Nothing is both a spoon and a fork! (At least not in this problem.) So "and" would have been inappropriate. There are no utensils that are spoons and forks. When we talk about the set of things that are A OR B, we mean that EACH of them may be EITHER A or B. That is, we are including in the set BOTH those that are A, AND those that are B. This is where the confusion and ambiguity come in! There are 8 utensils that are spoons or forks. Your son read it in a way that is commonly used in nontechnical English, taking "How many are A or B" to mean two separate questions combined: "How many are A, how many are B". I can see how that could be tempting in this case; the two numbers happen to be the same, so he could take the question to mean "How many are A (which is also the same as the number that are B". If there had been 3 spoons and 4 forks, that interpretation would not have made as much sense; the best answer he could give would be "3, or 4". We don't combine questions like that in math, to avoid confusion. So the book was right, but the question is ambiguous if the teacher has not taught (or does not know) the standard mathematical usage. (This usage is important in some later topics, such as probability, so it's definitely worth teaching.) If you have any further questions, feel free to write back. - Doctor Peterson, The Math Forum http://mathforum.org/dr.math/ Date: 01/23/2008 at 12:47:17 From: Doctor Riz Subject: Re: and/or Hi Mikki - I'd like to add one piece to what Dr. Peterson wrote. While this is a slightly different application of the idea, my students always found this particular example of the logical difference between AND and OR helpful. In logic, an AND statement is only true if both parts of it are true. If I say, "I am in Vermont AND I am in New Hampshire" the only way that can be true is if I am standing on the border with one foot in each state. An OR statement is true if either part is true. If I say, "I am in Vermont OR I am in New Hampshire" that statement is true as long as I am in either state (it's also true if I'm straddling the border). The only way an OR statement is not true is if both parts are false, such as if I were standing in Massachusetts when I made my statement about being in Vermont or New Hampshire. With your question about utensils being spoons OR forks, I count every utensil that is either a spoon or a fork, giving 8 of the 11. If I were asked what fraction of the utensils were spoons AND forks, there would be zero since the utensil would have to be both things. There IS a utensil you sometimes see in fast food places which is a spoon shape with teeth on the front edge, and it's generally referred to as a "spork", a combination of spoon and fork. That's what I'd need for a utensil to be considered a spoon AND a fork. Does that help? Write back if you have questions on any of this. - Doctor Riz, The Math Forum http://mathforum.org/dr.math/ Date: 01/23/2008 at 13:33:11 From: Mikki Subject: Thank you (and/or) Thank you both for your prompt responses. You have taught me something and definitely given me something to think about. Search the Dr. Math Library: Ask Dr. MathTM 1994-2013 The Math Forum The jigsaw can cut all those little shapes. Imagine you have two scoops of ice cream sitting on a plate. The first scoop is made by combining twice as much of one flavor of ice cream (let's say vanilla) in the bottom layer as there is of another flavor (say chocolate) in the top layer. This forms a dome shape. The second scoop is a flatter dome made by mixing equal amounts of the same two flavors. Now let's pretend these ice cream domes are transparent and stack them on top of each other. But wait! They're not exactly the same size, so they overlap creating a "lens" shaped area where you can see both domes at once. Your task is to figure out the volume of this lens-shaped section. To make things easier, imagine placing a round cookie cutter (the kind that matches our plate) into the overlapping part. By doing this, we now have three circular disks - one for the top scoop, one for the bottom scoop, and one for the overlap. These disks will help us visualize and calculate the volume of our lens-shaped section. The height of each disk in the lens-shape tells us how thick the ice cream is at that point. To find the total volume, we add up all those thin layers of ice cream from the cookie cutter at the top to the plate at the bottom. In math language, this means setting up a double integral. However, instead of x's and y's, think of them as labels for each tiny little disk going around in circles within our cookie cutter. First, we need to decide which ice cream scoop is on top and which is on bottom when adding up our volumes because we want our final answer to make sense (and be positive). Since the first scoop has more vanilla than the second, it should be the bottom scoop since it has a larger overall amount of ice cream. So, we'll subtract the volume of the smaller, second scoop from the bigger, first scoop. Now comes the tricky part: finding the limits or boundaries for our disks labeled by x's and y's. Going back to our cookie cutter, remember that its edge traces out the outermost part of our lens-shaped region. That curve marks the limit for both x and y. We can describe this boundary using the equation of the circle centered at the origin with radius 1 (since our plate has this size), which is x + y = 1. So, our setup looks like this: Volume = [(2x + y ) - (8 - x - 2y )] dx dy over the region bounded by x + y = 1. Finally, just like licking off the delicious ice cream starting from the rim towards the center, integrate the expression across the entire range of possible x and y values that fit within our cookie cutter! When a user submits an edit to a page, MediaWiki writes it to the database, but without deleting the previous versions of the page, thus allowing easy reverts in case of vandalism or spamming. MediaWiki can manage image and multimedia files, too, which are stored in the filesystem. For large wikis with lots of users, MediaWiki supports caching and can be easily coupled with Squid proxy server software. Title: How to Call Italy from Anywhere in the World Introduction: Making an international call can sometimes be intimidating due to the complexities involved in dialing foreign phone numbers. However, with this comprehensive guide, you will learn how to make calls to Italy effortlessly. This article covers various scenarios such as calling Italian landlines or mobile phones using different types of telephones (home phone, office phone, cellphone) and carriers (mobile network operators, VoIP providers). By following these steps carefully, you'll soon master the art of placing successful calls to Italy. Step 1: Determine Your Type of Phone & Carrier Firstly, identify whether you are using a home phone, office phone, or cellphone. Each type has slightly different procedures but they all share basic principles. Also, take note of your carrier because specific rules may apply depending on their services. Key Tips: - If you have a smartphone, consider using apps like Skype or WhatsApp that offer cheaper rates than traditional carriers. - Check if your carrier offers inclusive minutes for international calls. Some contracts include free calls to certain countries, which could save you money. Step 2: Find Out The Italian Number Details Obtain the full Italian number you wish to call. It should consist of ten digits - three for the area code starting with '0', followed by seven more for the local number. For example, (+39) 0XXX XXX XXYY. Note down the exact spelling of names too, especially when dealing with businesses or institutions. Guidelines: - Always ask permission before making any personal calls during work hours or using someone else's device. - Double-check the number to avoid misdials and unnecessary costs. Step 3: Understand International Dialing Procedures Every country has its own unique country calling code (CCC), which is a set of numerals used along with the national destination code (NDC) and subscriber number (SN) to form an international telephone number. Italy's CCC is +39. To make a call to Italy, replace '+' with the appropriate access code based on your location and device. Key Points: - An international call begins with dialling the exit code. From North America, press '011'; most other regions use '00'. Mobile users might simply need to hold down the '0' button until getting the plus sign instead. - After exiting your domestic network, enter Italy's CCC ('39'). - Omit the leading zero of the area code when including the CCC. Examples: - US residents would dial 011-39-XXX-XXXX-XXXX. - UK citizens would dial 00-39-XXX-XXXX-XXXX. - European mobile users holding down '0' get + then dial 39-XXX-XXXX-XXXX. Step 4: Place The Actual Call Now, gather everything together and input all required codes and numbers sequentially. Once connected, wait for the ring tone before hanging up prematurely. Remember, patience pays off! Additional Information: - Time differences matter; ensure you know what time it is in Italy to prevent waking people up at odd hours. - Be aware of potential language barriers; learning some common phrases could help smooth communication. Conclusion: By now, you should feel confident about calling Italy regardless of where you are located or what kind of phone you possess. With practice, even complicated tasks become second nature. So go ahead, pick up that phone, and enjoy connecting with friends, family, or colleagues across borders! oh, you're going to put something on top of your muffins? Choosing a name after a female-to-male (FTM) sex change can be an exciting and significant part of your transition journey. This decision allows you to align your identity with your true self, fostering confidence and authenticity. However, this process may also feel overwhelming due to the infinite possibilities and personal significance attached to names. To help guide you through this important milestone, we have compiled a comprehensive, 12-step tutorial. ### Step 1: Reflect on Your Identity Firstly, consider what aspects of your personality, history, or values resonate most deeply with you when thinking about potential new names. By identifying core elements that define who you are, you will find inspiration for selecting a moniker that reflects your genuine self. Key tip: Consider seeking input from trusted friends, family members, or mental health professionals familiar with your experiences during this stage of reflection. ### Step 2: Research Cultural Significance If there is a particular cultural background associated with your gender identity, research common male names within those communities as potential options. Honoring one's heritage can provide a sense of belonging and continuity throughout the transition process. Guideline: Always ensure any chosen name respectfully acknowledges its origins and avoids appropriation. ### Step 3: Explore Meanings Behind Names Delve into the meanings behind various names, focusing on those that hold special significance or relevance to your life story. A meaningful connection to your new name can foster pride, ownership, and empowerment during your FTM transition. For example, if nature holds great importance for you, seek out names inspired by flora, fauna, or celestial bodies. ### Step 4: Contemplate Length & Pronunciation Take note of the length and pronunciation of potential names, aiming for simplicity and ease of enunciation. Shorter names tend to be more accessible and memorable, while complex pronunciations could lead to miscommunication or misunderstandings. Additionally, evaluate how well the name flows off the tongue when combined with your surname(s). ### Step 5: Experiment with Different Options To narrow down your list of contenders, practice saying each name aloud and observe how they sound and feel in different contexts - such as introductions, conversations, or professional settings. Eliminate any names that don't sit comfortably or seem unnatural to you. Key tip: Try writing down your top choices and visualizing them alongside your signature; this exercise can offer valuable insight into whether a name feels like a proper fit. ### Step 6: Seek Feedback From Trusted Individuals Well let's finish and see what else is in here first. Part of an organism's DNA is "non-coding DNA" sequences. They do not code for protein sequences. Some noncoding DNA is transcribed into non-coding RNA molecules, such as transfer RNA, ribosomal RNA, and regulatory RNAs. Other sequences are not transcribed at all, or give rise to RNA of unknown function. The amount of non-coding DNA varies greatly among species. Title: How to Perform a 360-Degree Spin on a Sanchez in Grand Theft Auto: Vice City Stories Grand Theft Auto: Vice City Stories (GTA VCS) is an action-adventure game that offers various vehicles for players to explore the open world. One thrilling stunt you can perform in the game is doing a 360-degree spin on a Sanchez, which is an off-road motorbike. This tutorial will guide you through the process with clear instructions, detailed explanations, and helpful tips. **Step 1:** Acquire a Sanchez First, you need access to a Sanchez motorcycle. You can find them scattered throughout the map or steal one from a gang member riding it. Once you have obtained a Sanchez, make sure you are in a spacious area free from obstacles like trees, buildings, or other vehicles. A large parking lot, field, or beach would be ideal. Why this step matters: Choosing the right location ensures your safety during the maneuver and allows enough space for error without causing damage to the bike or yourself. Key tip: Save your progress before attempting any advanced moves; if something goes wrong, you won't lose much time reloading your saved game. **Step 2:** Position Yourself Correctly Before initiating the spin, position the Sanchez so that its front wheel points directly towards where you want to rotate. Ideally, face perpendicular to a straight line, such as the edge of a building or curbstone. Make sure there's ample room behind you too. Title: Draw a Dog's Head Using the Line Method 1. **Understand the Basics**: Before you start drawing, it is essential to understand that this guide uses the line method. This means we will be using lines and shapes as building blocks to create the dog's head. The final image will have most of these lines removed or blended into shadows once the basic structure has been established. 2. **Sketch an Oval Shape**: Begin by lightly sketching an oval shape on your paper. This forms the foundation for the snout area of your dog's head. Make sure the height-to-width ratio isn't too extreme; aim for about 3:4. It should look like an egg standing upright on its narrower end. 3. **Add Two Circles**: Above the center of the oval, draw two circles intersecting with each other - one slightly larger than the other. These form the basis for the eyes. Remember, they don't need to be perfect; real dogs come in all shapes and sizes! 4. **Define the Muzzle**: Now, extend two curved lines downwards from the bottom part of the oval. They should converge towards where the lower eye circle meets the oval. This defines the muzzle or snout of the dog. 5. **Outline Ears**: On either side of the head, above the upper eye circle, draw two floppy ear shapes. For now, keep them simple and roughly symmetrical. We can add more detail later. 6. **Mark Nose & Mouth**: At the end of the muzzle, mark a small dot for the nose. Just below this, make another horizontal guideline to represent the mouth line. 7. **Detail Eyes**: Go back to those initial circular guides for the eyes. Flesh out the top halves to become eyebrows, then give definition to the bottom parts which form the lower eyelids. Connect these sections smoothly but ensure there's still some emptiness left inside to maintain the illusion of depth. 8. **Refine Ears**: Erase any unnecessary construction lines within the ears. Then, subdivide the inner surface of each ear into three segments vertically. Add slight curves to suggest natural folding of the ear leather. 9. **Shape the Forehead**: Notice how the forehead looks flat due to our current outline? To fix this, redraw the outline such that the skull bulges outward gently at the crown while tapering softly towards the back of the head. 10. **Blend Lines & Shadows**: Once satisfied with the overall shape, begin erasing visible construction lines gradually. As you do so, try shading certain areas simultaneously. Use light strokes to mimic fur texture, focusing on adding shadow around crevices (like between the ears) and under protrusions (such as the brow bone). 1 It looks like the little policeman hurt his foot because he's using the branch to walk like a cane. The "Iglesia y Monasterio de San Francisco" (English: Church and Monastery of St. Francis), colloquially known as El San Francisco, is a colonial-styled church and monastery in Quito, Ecuador. Construction of the building began a few weeks after the founding of the city in 1534 and ended in 1604. The founder of the church was Franciscan missionary Jodoco Ricke. [continues to position the clothing for SHN] Changes tone at that can be dangerous Many children have problems mixing up letters like b and d, p and q, m and n, and other letters. How do children learn letters? The common-sense view that we learn letters by memorizing their shapes turns out to be wrong. A letter is recognized by its sequence of features, not as a whole shape. For example, letter m is made with a short line down, followed by two humps to the right. Since letter recognition depends on understanding a sequence of features, the best way to teach children the sequence of features in making a letter is by guiding printing practice. For instance, it is extraordinarily helpful for children confusing letters b and d to practice printing d in the sequence, "First little c, then little d." Sue Dickson, creator of the program Sing, Spell, Read, and Write, has developed many such helpful adages to guide printing practice with letters. I have transcribed her list below. Here is a diagram showing the Zaner-Bloser directions for printing letters: Using primary paper with guidelines helps children learn the relative size and position of letter features. Using plain paper is not "creative"; it makes it harder for children to learn the relative sizes and positions of the features. Young children learning letters need vivid, concrete language to remember the abstract features that make up letters. It helps to name the lines on primary paper. I like calling the top line the "rooftop," the dotted crossing line the "fence," the bottom line the "sidewalk," and the space beneath the sidewalk the "ditch." Others like to use indoor names, such as the ceiling, windows, floor, and basement. The script below suggests ways to use vivid, concrete language to guide children as they form letters. For example, to make the lower-case b, start at the rooftop, drop straight down to the sidewalk, and then b-b-bounce back up to the fence and around. Briefly, you drop down, bounce up, and around. After making a few letters with the teacher's guidance, children need to talk themselves through the formation of letter features, saying to themselves, "Drop down, bounce up, and around." Daily writing opportunities encourage children to invent spellings, a key innovation from whole language. When students stretch pronunciations, they identify phonemes and symbolize them with letters, providing valuable practice with phoneme awareness. In a landmark study (Clarke, 1988), children who devised invented spellings during first grade were better than children who were provided spellings in word recognition and in spelling. The reason they were better is that constant practice inventing spellings developed their phoneme awareness, allowing them to better understand phonics and spelling instruction. Invented spelling is particularly important with children initially low in phoneme awareness. Children who are aware of phonemes do not need to invent spellings since they already possess the chief benefit of this activity. We want students to write for communicative purposes, to tell us something they think is important. The urge to communicate is the prime motive for writing. We will greatly reduce children's output (and thus their phoneme awareness practice) if we insist on turning message writing into an exercise, e.g., by requiring certain words. Message writing is not the time to teach standard spelling. After a child has written, simply appreciate the message and respond to the ideas. Have the student read you the message, and if the spelling departs significantly from standard spelling, transcribe it below the original message. This will not hurt the child's feelings; it says, "What you wrote is so important that I want to write in grown-up writing to remember exactly what it says." Be sure to give specific praise (e.g., for a phonemic spelling or well-turned phrase) and post the message prominently. To get the message started, you will want to suggest topics your student could write about. The topic suggestions might grow from a conversation during your initial greeting to the student; you may hear of a sports triumph, a family outing, or the antics of a baby sister. Upcoming holidays (especially Halloween, Christmas, Valentines Day, Easter, and Independence Day) often capture a child's imagination and make good writing topics. You can also encourage writing by putting up an illustration. Pictures can make a powerful stimulus to the imagination (which is why they are used in projective tests). You can model the form and content of a message by writing messages to your student. You may want to make a cardstock "mailbox" to send and receive messages. The following examples show the kind of concrete and colorful talk that make letter forms memorable to children and guide their printing and letter recognition: For capital A, start at the rooftop, go down the slide to the sidewalk, then down the slide the other way, and cross at the fence. For lowercase a, don't start at the fence. Start under the fence. Go up and touch the fence, then around and touch the sidewalk, around and straight down. For capital B, go straight down to the sidewalk, around for his big chest, and around for his big tummy. For lowercase b, start at the roof, go down, b-b-bounce up and around. For capital C, start just below the rooftop, go up to touch, around, and up. For lowercase c, start like little a: Go up and touch the fence, then around and up. For capital D, start at the roof, go straight down, pick up, and go around. For lowercase d, first little c, then little d. For capital E, go down for a strong backbone, over for his hat, over for his belt, over for his shoes. For lowercase e, get in the center of the space below the fence; go toward the door (or window), up to touch the fence, around and up. For capital F, go down, over for his hat, over for his belt (but no shoes). For lowercase f, start to make a little c up in the air, then straighten it out, go down, and cross at the fence. For capital G, form a big C, then come back to the line to give him a tray to hold straight. For lowercase g, first make a, then, gee, that's a good idea: If the ball falls, it falls into the basket. For capital H, down for a wall, down for a wall, then cross at the fence. For lowercase h, start at the rooftop, come down, and hump over. For capital I, start with a straight back, then give him his headdress and his moccasins. For lowercase i, go down from the fence, and give him a feather. For capital J, go down, and turn to make a basket, and put his hat on. For lowercase j, start at the fence, go down through the sidewalk, and turn the same way, and give him a dot. For capital K, go down, come out here, into the center, and down to the sidewalk. Lowercase k is just as tall as his daddy. Start at the rooftop, go down, pick up at the fence, into the center and down. For capital L, go down and turn the corner. For lowercase l, just a straight line down from the rooftop to the sidewalk. For capital M, go down straight, down the slide, up the slide, and down straight. For lowercase m, go down, hump around, hump around. For capital N, go down straight, down the slide, down straight. For lowercase n, go down, up, and hump over. For capital O, always form a C first, and then close it up. For lowercase o, same way: first a little c and close it up. For capital P, go down, pick up, and around to the fence. For lowercase p, start at the fence, go straight down into the ditch, come up and put his chin on the sidewalk. For capital Q, first make a big O, and give the queen her walking stick. For lowercase q, start with an a, come down, and give the queen some curly hair. For capital R, down, pick up, and around to the fence, then slant down. For lowercase r, down, up, and hook over. For capital S, first form a c up in the air between the rooftop and the fence, then swing back. For lowercase s, form a tiny c up in the air, and then swing back. For capital T, go down and cross at the top. Lowercase t is just a teenager, not as tall as his daddy, but not short either; cross at the fence. For capital U, down, curve, and up (no stem). For lowercase u, down, curve up, and straight down for a stem. For capital V, slant down and up. For lowercase v, slant down and up. For capital W, slant down, up, down, up. For lowercase w, down, up, down, up. For capital X, down on a slant, pick up, back in the other direction. For lowercase x, down and back. For capital Y, start with a v up in the air, and put a stem on it. For lowercase y, go down on a slant, pick up your pencil, slant down, touch, and on into the ditch. For capital Z, make a 7, and then go back. For lowercase z, make a little 7, and then go back. Click here to return to the Reading Along the wall who tiptoes up the garden with a pitter-patter? Sumerian/Grammar/Lesson Three - The Genitive What is the Genitive Case? The "genitive case" is nothing more than a way for a language to describe an "of" relationship. For instance, "the king of Ur" is a genitive construction. So is "the father of Sargon". No need to be scared of the terminology - linguists just like using fancy words to describe other types of fancy words. The genitive in Sumerian The genitive case is used heavily in Sumerian, and has several different forms. The simplest form is to suffix a two noun compound with the .ak particle. (Remember particles? They're the little things we tack on to words or phrases to modify the meaning of that phrase.) Let's look at a couple examples. Consider the phrase lugal Urim.ak. First things first, we figure out our vocabulary. We remember that lugal means king, and Urim is the city we call Ur. So we have two nouns, and we also notice our new friend, the .ak particle. This tells us right away that we're looking at a genitive construction. So put it all together, and we get: - lugal Urim.ak = the king of Ur Pretty simple, right? It really is that easy! Just remember to be on the lookout for that little .ak particle, and you'll be able to spot a genitive a mile away. Here's another example: dumu nin Lagas.ak.ak. As usual, we find our vocabulary first. Of course, dumu means child, and nin means lady or queen. Lagas is just the city we call Lagash. Now at the end of this little phrase, we see something interesting: two little .ak particles! What could this mean? Well, just like in English, where you can say the son of the neighbor of the chairman of the board of the company, in Sumerian we can string multiple genitives together as well. In Sumerian, though, we unravel multiple genitive constructions from inside out, so we might read this example as: - dumu nin Lagas.ak.ak = dumu [nin [Lagas.ak].ak] = [the child of [the queen of Lagash]] = the child of the queen of Lagash Nothing really new, we just put two genitive constructions back to back. You should probably have the idea by now, so we'll see if you can follow along with a little quick quiz. Make sure you understand what the following little phrases mean. If you're having trouble, you can hover over the text to get a translation. - . . Dropping the final 'k' This is all well and good, and on paper everything looks good. But what about on clay tablets? Well, it turns out that an often seen feature of spoken Sumerian was dropping phrase-final sounds off of case particles. Don't be scared by how technical that sounds - it happens in English, too! At least in America, we have a tendency to drop the final 'g' from the '-ing' suffix, so we might pronounce 'going' as 'goin', or 'digging' as 'diggin'. Simple, right? Just drop a final sound from a particle. In this case, the Sumerian genitive, our particle is .ak, and hence ends in a /k/ sound. In real written Sumerian, we often see phrases like lugal Urim.ak actually written as lu-gal Urim-ma, with no written acknowledgment of the final /k/ sound from the genitive particle. But don't worry! It turns out that much of the time you'll find another case particle tacked on to the end of a genitive phrase, in which case the /k/ sound is in fact pronounced. For instance, if our phrase above were in the ergative case (which is kind of like the subject of a transitive sentence, more later), then we would add the particle .e to the end of the phrase, so we would have lugal Urim.ak.e, which would be written lu-gal Urim-ma-ke, with a pronounced /k/. Even if you don't have other clues like this, you'll quickly see that context will normally erase any ambiguity in translation. Okay now how many have I got? [MOT has lined up the silver men and two nuts] And tell me did you get dressed up? It's [camera] not even on you [Chi]. Why do we use this and these? We use this (singular) and these (plural) as pronouns: - to talk about people or things near us: This is a nice cup of tea. Whose shoes are these? - to introduce people: This is Janet. These are my friends, John and Michael. We don't say These are John and Michael. We say This is John and this is Michael. - to introduce ourselves to begin a conversation on the phone: Hello, this is David, Can I speak to Sally? Why do we use that and those? We use that (singular) and those (plural): - to talk about things that are not near us: This is our house, and that's Rebecca's house over there. Those are very expensive shoes. - We also use that to refer back to something someone said or did: - - Shall we go to the cinema? - Yes, that's a good idea. - - I've got a new job. - That's great. - - I'm very tired. - Why is that? this, these, that, those with nouns We also use this, these, that and those with nouns to show proximity We use this and these for people or things near us: We have lived in this house for twenty years. Have you read all of these books? and that and those for things that are not near us: Who lives in that house? Who are those people? - Determiners and quantifiers - Clause, phrase and sentence Tags for teachers A - Z of Content - 1 of 6 [Disney Phone says "yep you guessed it I'm Goofy"] Raster images are made up of very small points called pixels. Digital cameras create raster pictures but artists can make them with computers too. The artists don't have to change a single pixel at a time - raster programs often have tools like paintbrushes, paint buckets and erasers to make a picture. Programs used to make these include Adobe Photoshop, GIMP and Corel Paint Shop Pro. Title: How to Get Over the Ending of Books Introduction: Finishing a beloved book can be an emotional experience, especially when the ending doesn't meet your expectations or leaves you feeling unsatisfied. This guide will help you navigate through those feelings and provide strategies to move forward. It is important to remember that everyone processes their emotions differently, so take what resonates with you and leave behind what doesn't. Step 1: Allow Yourself to Experience the Emotions When you finish a book and feel disappointed or upset about the ending, allow yourself to fully experience these emotions without judgment. Acknowledge them as valid and understandable reactions to something significant you have invested time and emotion into. By accepting your feelings, you create space for healing and moving past the initial reaction. Key tip: Reflect on why the ending affected you this way - was it unexpected? Did it contradict your personal values? Understanding the root cause may aid closure. Step 2: Discuss With Others Talking about the book and sharing your thoughts with others can offer new perspectives and insights that might change your viewpoint on the ending. Join online communities, forums, or local book clubs where people discuss books openly. Hearing other interpretations could lead to appreciation of aspects you hadn't considered before. Guideline: Be open-minded during discussions; other readers may bring up points that didn't cross your mind initially. Step 3: Look For Alternative Endings Sometimes, authors write alternative endings or fans create their own versions based on the storyline. Exploring these alternatives can give you a sense of resolution if the original left you hanging. Keep in mind though, they are subjective and should be taken lightly. Step 4: Create Your Own Ending If no satisfying alternate endings exist, consider writing one yourself! Putting words down gives you control over the narrative and lets you explore various outcomes. Plus, it serves as a creative outlet for processing your feelings. Step 5: Seek Solace in Other Media Distracting yourself with similar stories in different formats (movies, TV shows, comics) can assist in getting over the book's conclusion since they often present diverse narratives around common themes. You never know-you might discover another favorite! Step 6: Practice Self-Care Reading is an intimate activity, forming strong connections between reader and characters. When such ties break due to dissatisfying conclusions, practice self-care by engaging in activities that nourish your mental health. Exercise regularly, eat well, sleep enough, meditate, or spend quality time with loved ones to rebalance your mood. Key Tip: Engage in hobbies that make you happy. The goal here is to remind yourself there's more joy outside the disappointing reading experience. Step 7: Reevaluate Your Reading Preferences After some distance from the emotional impact, reassess whether specific genres, styles, or tropes consistently trigger disappointment. If certain patterns emerge, adjust your future reads accordingly. There's nothing wrong with exploring new literary territories. Conclusion: Moving past the ending of a book takes time but implementing these steps gradually can ease the process. Remember, every read enriches us somehow - even frustrating ones teach valuable lessons about our tastes and preferences. Happy reading! Title: How to Execute a Left or Right Facing Movement Executing left or right facing movements is an essential skill in various activities such as dancing, martial arts, exercise routines, and even in everyday life. This tutorial will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to perform these movements effectively and safely. **Step 1: Understand the Concept** Left or right-facing movements involve shifting your body weight from one foot to another while turning your upper body towards the desired direction (left or right). The basic principle behind this movement lies in pivoting on the balls of both feet, maintaining balance throughout the process. It's crucial to understand that executing these movements requires control over your core muscles, leg strength, and understanding of body alignment. **Key Tips:** * Always maintain proper posture during execution. * Engage your abdominal muscles for better stability and control. * Keep your knees slightly bent to absorb shock and improve flexibility. **Step 2: Prepare Your Body** Before attempting any left or right facing movements, ensure your body is warmed up properly. Start by doing some light cardio exercises like jogging or jumping jacks for about five minutes to increase blood flow. Follow this with dynamic stretches targeting major muscle groups including hamstrings, quadriceps, calves, hips, and lower back. Stretching enhances mobility, reduces injury risk, and readies the mind for focused practice. **Key Guidelines:** * Never stretch cold muscles; always warm up first. * Hold each stretch for at least 30 seconds without bouncing. * Gradually deepen each stretch to avoid straining the muscles. **Step 3: Master Basic Footwork** The foundation of successful left or right facing movements starts with mastering fundamental footwork patterns. Begin practicing by standing straight with feet hip-width apart. Shift your weight onto your left foot, then slide your right foot outwards making sure its heel doesn't touch the ground. Pivot on the ball of your right foot until your toes point in the new direction. Reverse the steps to return to the starting position. Practice several times until comfortable with the motion. **Key Points:** * Distribute your weight evenly between both legs initially. * Move smoothly without jerking motions. * Maintain eye contact with a fixed point ahead to help keep balance. **Step 4: Add Upper Body Rotation** Once you have grasped the basics of footwork, proceed to incorporate upper body rotation. As you shift your weight and turn your lower body, twist your torso simultaneously so that your shoulders face the same way as your turned foot. Remember to keep your arms relaxed but ready to move along with your body. **Important Note:** Ensure your spine remains upright during rotation. Avoid leaning forward or backward which can disrupt balance and lead to improper form. **Step 5: Refine Your Technique** After combining lower and upper body movements, refine your technique through repetition. Focus on smooth transitions, controlled speed, and correct alignment. Practicing slowing down and gradually increasing speed will enhance precision and fluidity. **Helpful Hints:** * Use mirrors or record yourself to observe and adjust your form. * Seek feedback from trainers or experienced individuals. * Consistently review progress and strive for continuous improvement. By following these steps meticulously, you should be able to execute accurate left or right facing movements confidently. Regular practice combined with patience and determination leads to noticeable improvements. Happy training! Title: How to Be Friends with Someone Rich Introduction: This guide is intended to provide helpful advice on building friendships with people who have wealth. It's important to note that this article does not advocate seeking friendships based solely on someone's financial status but rather offers guidance on navigating such relationships when they occur naturally. Remember, genuine friendship should always be the ultimate goal, regardless of one's socio-economic background. Step 1: Identify Potential Friendship Opportunities Key Tip: Keep an open mind about forming friendships; don't limit yourself to those from similar economic backgrounds. To begin, you need to meet potential friends who are wealthy. You can do this by participating in activities or joining groups where affluent individuals might be present. This could include charity events, exclusive clubs, high-end fitness centers, or professional networking gatherings. However, ensure your primary motivation is not their wealth but rather shared interests or goals. Step 2: Establish Common Ground Guideline: Focus on mutual interests, values, and experiences to foster connection. Once you've identified possible friends, engage them in conversations around common topics of interest. Shared hobbies, travel destinations, books, movies, or even opinions on current affairs can serve as excellent starting points. Building a relationship on these foundations will make it more likely to last beyond initial interactions. Step 3: Show Genuine Interest Tip: Ask thoughtful questions and listen attentively to show you value their insights and perspectives. Demonstrate sincere curiosity about their life, work, and passions. People appreciate when others take an active interest in getting to know them better. Avoid asking intrusive questions about income or assets, which may come off as insincere or opportunistic. Step 4: Respect Their Time and Space Caution: Don't overwhelm them with constant communication or demands for attention. Wealthy individuals often lead busy lives. Respect their time by being punctual for meetings and not expecting instant responses to messages. Give them space if they seem preoccupied or unavailable. Overstepping boundaries can quickly sour a budding friendship. Step 5: Offer Value Without Expectation Advice: Contribute something meaningful to the relationship without anticipating anything in return. Every friendship should involve some form of reciprocity. While you shouldn't expect monetary favors, offering your skills, knowledge, or resources can help balance the relationship. For instance, you could offer assistance with personal tasks, introduce them to new contacts, or share interesting articles related to their business. Step 6: Maintain Boundaries Alert: Beware of crossing ethical lines or engaging in dishonest behavior for personal gain. Friendship doesn't give you license to exploit their generosity or resources. Refrain from using their connections for selfish purposes or sharing confidential information. Unethical actions will damage trust and potentially end the friendship. Step 7: Handle Financial Differences Gracefully Suggestion: When disparities arise, handle situations tactfully to preserve harmony. There may be instances where your financial situation differs greatly from yours. Whether it's choosing restaurants, planning trips, or discussing careers, approach these differences delicately. If necessary, express gratitude for any generosity but assert your independence by suggesting alternatives that fit within your budget. Conclusion: Forming friendships organically, respecting individual boundaries, and contributing meaningfully are essential elements of building lasting relationships - including ones with wealthy individuals. By following this guide, you'll increase your chances of developing authentic friendships grounded in mutual respect and understanding. [pats the sheep to demonstrate for GAR] The people are too big to fit in there. Building a good career in the IT field requires a combination of education, skills, experience, and networking. Here's a step-by-step guide to help you get started: **Step 1: Choose an Area of Interest** The IT field is vast and includes many different areas such as software development, network administration, database management, cybersecurity, data science, and more. To build a successful career, it's essential to choose an area that interests you and has growth potential. Research various IT fields, consider your strengths and passions, and select one that aligns with them. *Key tip:* Don't be afraid to explore multiple areas before making a decision. You may find that your interest lies at the intersection of several disciplines. **Step 2: Acquire Necessary Education and Skills** Once you have chosen an area of interest, focus on acquiring the necessary education and skills. This could include earning a bachelor's or master's degree in computer science or a related field, obtaining certifications, or completing online courses. *Key guideline:* Always stay up-to-date with the latest technologies and trends in your chosen area. Attend conferences, read industry publications, and participate in relevant online communities. **Step 3: Gain Experience** To build a solid foundation in the IT field, gaining hands-on experience is crucial. Consider internships, part-time jobs, or volunteer work while pursuing your education. Once you graduate, look for entry-level positions that offer opportunities for growth and learning. *Key tip:* Focus on building a strong portfolio showcasing your skills and achievements. Participate in open-source projects, contribute to online forums, or create personal projects that demonstrate your expertise. **Step 4: Develop Soft Skills** While technical skills are important, soft skills like communication, teamwork, problem-solving, and leadership can set you apart from other candidates. Look for ways to develop these skills through extracurricular activities, volunteering, or participating in group projects. *Key guideline:* Practice active listening, clear communication, and empathy when working with others. These qualities will make you a valuable asset to any team. **Step 5: Network** Networking is vital in the IT field. Attend industry events, join professional organizations, and connect with professionals on LinkedIn. Engage in meaningful conversations, ask questions, and seek advice from those who have experience in your chosen area. *Key tip:* Offer value to others by sharing knowledge, insights, or resources. People are more likely to remember and engage with those who provide genuine assistance. **Step 6: Advance Your Career** As you gain experience and develop your skills, look for opportunities to advance your career. Seek out promotions, pursue higher-paying roles, or start your own business. Continuously evaluate your goals and adjust your path accordingly. *Key guideline:* Be proactive about seeking new challenges and taking calculated risks. Embrace failure as an opportunity to learn and grow. By following these steps, you can build a fulfilling and rewarding career in the IT field. Remember to always stay curious, keep learning, and maintain a positive attitude. Success will follow! Are you through playin' with the barn? XXX oh I bet you'll like this. Let's Learn About Weather! - Grades: 1-2 - Unit Plan: - Observe weather. - Listen to weather stories. - Recognize different kinds of weather. - Write about weather. - Large paper cloud for brainstorming words - Observation notebook for each students - Cloudy with a Chance of Meatballs by Judi Barrett, available in the Teacher Store - What Will the Weather Be Like Today? by Paul Rogers, available in the Teacher Store - Weather Words and What They Mean by Gail Gibbons, available in the Teacher Store - Who Cares About the Weather? by Melvin Berger, Natalie Lunis Set Up and Prepare - Cut out and post a large paper cloud. - Make observation notebooks for each student. I use several sheets of paper that have a few lines for writing on the bottom and space for drawing a picture on the top. Add a cover sheet with a cloud. The students can write "Weather" in the cloud. Step 1: Read Cloudy with a Chance of Meatballs by Judi Barrett. Step 2: Brainstorm words students may want to use when writing about weather. These can be written on the large paper cloud. Step 3: Have the students write a new page in the style of Cloudy with a Chance of Meatballs. Bind the pages into a book. Step 1: Read What Will the Weather Be Like Today? by Paul Rogers and Weather Words and What They Mean by Gail Gibbons. Step 2: Discuss types of weather. Add weather words to the word cloud. Step 3: Have the students draw the weather they see outside the classroom in their observation notebook and write words or sentences that describe it. Step 1: Read Who Cares About the Weather? by Melvin Berger and Natalie Lunis. Step 2: Have the students write and illustrate a page for the class' own book, Who Cares About the Weather? Bind the pages into a book. Learn more about plants and play plant games with these fun Web sites. - Dan's Wild Weather page http://www.wildwildweather.com/ Tell someone at home three facts about weather. Tomorrow, be ready to share what you said with your table partner. (The next day, the teacher will walk around the classroom listening to what students are sharing and make comments. Then the students will be asked to share with a different partner and listen to what that person said to someone at home.) - Was there enough time? - Were the students successful or frustrated observing the weather? - Were the students able to combine weather elements such as cold "and" windy? - Were the students able to write about weather without a lot of help? - Could the students tell me about weather? - How many students were able to identify a type of weather? - How many students were also able to describe the weather in their observation notebooks? Copies of the students' observation notebooks will be saved for their assessment portfolios. Growing Independence and Fluency In order for students to learn how to read faster, smoother, and more expressively, they must become fluent Fluency refers to a student's ability to read words accurately and automatically. This lesson focuses on students' development of the read quickly, smoothly, and expressively. When a student accomplishes tasks, he or she has gained fluency. In this lesson, fluency is gained repeated reading, timed reading, and one-minute reads. * "The dog plays all day long in the written on the board * Laminated cut outs of Speedy Gonzalez Velcro on the back * A laminated picture of a dusty pathway (like that is numbered from one end to the other with a block of cheese and a the end and pieces of Velcro by each number * Class set of the book of What Will we are going to work on a reading skill called fluency. Fluency is when you can read fast, smoothly, and also with expression. If we want to become fluent readers we must practice a lot though. I am going to read this sentence on the board without fluency. "Th-e a-ll d-ay i-n th-e p-a-r-k." That was pretty hard to understand wasn't it? Now I am going to read it fluently. (Point to each word as I read). "The dog plays all day in the park." That was a lot easier to understand and it was faster, right? So we see how important it is for us to be able to read fluently. Sometimes when we are reading, we will come to words that we do not know. When that happens, remember to use your cover up critter, or cross check by reading the rest of the sentence, or pick out the vowel sound first and then read the beginning of the word and put it all together" (model each of these methods when explaining them). - Split the class up into pairs. Pass out to each student: a copy of the book What Will the Seal Eat, a Speed Record Sheet, a Fluency Literary Rubric, and a laminated Speedy Gonzalez cut out. - I will explain to the students that one person is going to be the "reader" and the other is going to be the "recorder." After the first person has read, they will switch jobs. Then I will tell them that they will start at the beginning of the book and read for one minute. I will be in charge of starting the stopwatch and telling the "reader" when to stop after one minute. When I say stop, the reader will put his/her finger on the word they were on. The "recorder" will count the words that the "reader" read and then record them on the Speed Record Sheet. The "reader" will now move their Speedy Gonzalez up to the number on the pathway that matches the number of words they read. The "recorder" will also fill in the Fluency Literary Rubric by coloring in the circles that describe how the "reader" did. They will then switch roles and the "reader" becomes the "recorder." They will then follow the same steps in their new jobs. - Allow the students to repeat this three times, in order to compare their results. - After every pair of students has completed the "one minute reads" three times, I will read the rest of the book to the class in case they didn't get to finish it during their minute reads. - Then we will have a discussion about the book, in order to make sure they comprehended what they read individually and what I read. - For assessment, I will take up the Speed Record Sheet and the Fluency Literary Rubric. I will compare the first and last readings because they should have increased with each time. Barrowclough, Lauren. Ready, Set, Let's Cushman, Shelia and Kornblum, Rona. What Will the Seal Eat? Chi I think they want you to speed it up here okay? Sickle Cell Disease What is sickle cell disease? Sickle cell disease changes normal, round red blood cells into cells that can be shaped like crescent moons. The name "sickle cell?? comes from the crescent shape of the cells. A sickle is a farm tool with a curved blade that can cut crops like wheat. Normal red blood cells move easily through your blood vessels, taking oxygen to every part of your body. But sickled cells can get stuck and block blood vessels, which stops the oxygen from getting through. That can cause a lot of pain. It can also harm organs, muscles, and bones. See a picture of sickle cells blocking a blood vessel. Having sickle cell disease means a lifelong battle against the health problems it can cause, such as pain, infections, anemia, and stroke. But many people are able to have a very good quality of life by learning to manage the disease. What causes sickle cell disease? Sickle cell disease is inherited, which means it is passed from parent to child. To get sickle cell disease, a child has to inherit two sickle cell genes-one from each parent. When a child inherits the gene from just one parent, that child has sickle cell trait. Having this trait means that you do not have the disease but you are a carrier and could pass the gene on to your children. What are the symptoms? Painful events are the most common symptom of sickle cell disease. They are periods of pain that happen when sickled cells get stuck in blood vessels and block the blood flow. These events usually cause pain in the hands, feet, belly, back, or chest. The pain may last for hours or for days. People with sickle cell disease often have anemia, caused by a shortage of red blood cells. Anemia makes you feel weak and tired. People with sickle cell anemia may look pale or washed out. Their skin and the whites of their eyes may have a yellowish look (jaundice). Doctors cannot yet tell which symptoms a child born with sickle cell disease will have, when they will start, or how serious they will be. How is sickle cell disease diagnosed? A simple blood test can show whether a person has sickle cell disease. Most states test for sickle cell disease before infants go home from the hospital. How is it treated? Managing pain is often a big part of having sickle cell disease. You can prepare for a painful event ahead of time by creating a pain management plan with your doctor. The plan should include what you can do at home to relieve pain for yourself or your child. The plan should also tell you when it is best to call a doctor or go to a hospital. Some people need regular blood transfusions to lower the risk of stroke and treat anemia and other problems. Regular checkups are an important part of life with this disease. People with sickle cell disease need a good working relationship with a doctor who is an expert in treating it. How do you manage life with sickle cell disease? [Me has thrown the ball, it bounces off something and goes onto the floor] Although Hugo's many talents did not include exceptional musical ability, he nevertheless had a great impact on the music world through the endless inspiration that his works provided for composers of the 19th and 20th century. Hugo himself particularly enjoyed the music of Gluck and Weber and greatly admired Beethoven, and rather unusually for his time, he also appreciated works by composers from earlier centuries such as Palestrina and Monteverdi. Two famous musicians of the 19th century were friends of Hugo: Berlioz and Liszt. The latter played Beethoven in Hugo's home, and Hugo joked in a letter to a friend that thanks to Liszt's piano lessons, he learned how to play a favourite song on the piano - even though only with one finger! Hugo also worked with composer Louise Bertin, writing the libretto for her 1836 opera "La Esmeralda" which was based on the character in "The Hunchback of Notre Dame". Although for various reasons the opera closed soon after its fifth performance and is little known today, it has been recently enjoying a revival, both in a piano/song concert version by Liszt at the Festival international Victor Hugo et gaux 2007 and in a full orchestral version to be presented in July 2008 at Le Festival de Radio France et Montpellier Languedoc-Roussillon. The Voyager 1 spacecraft found a hexagonal cloud pattern near Saturn's north pole at about 78 N. The Cassini-Huygens probe later confirmed it in 2006. Unlike the north pole, the south pole does not show any hexagonal cloud feature. The probe also discovered a hurricane-like storm locked to the south pole that clearly showed an eyewall. There was no answer. The robot marched across the room and found a little boy. He asked the boy, "May I clap with you?" The boy smiled and nodded. The robot and the boy started to clap together, their hands becoming faster and faster. They laughed and smiled, happy that they could play this simple game. As they clapped, the robot noticed something extraordinary. Where his hands touched the soft pillow, the pillow began to move. He couldn't believe it. He looked over to the boy with a shocked expression and said, "Did you see that?" The boy looked down at the pillow, a huge grin spreading across his face. He said to the robot, "I think you have brought the pillow to life!" The robot's eyes lit up. He couldn't believe what he had done. The robot and the little boy kept clapping, faster and faster, until the pillow began to move and dance around the room! The robot had given life to the soft pillow through his joyful clapping. Once upon a time, there was a curious cow who was always looking for something new. One day the cow decided to explore beyond its usual boundaries. But what the cow didn't know was that the new path ahead would separate it from the other cows for a very long time. The cow kept going, more curious than ever before. As it walked further away, the other cows noticed and started to call out. But the cow kept walking and eventually the sound of the other cows faded away. Everything seemed unfamiliar, but the cow was determined to keep going. Unfortunately, the cow didn't realize how far it had gone and before long it was completely lost. The cow looked around, but all the scenery seemed strange and unfamiliar. Then, just as the cow started to worry, it heard the sound of the other cows again. The cow followed the sound and eventually it found its way back to where it had started. The cow was relieved to be back with the other cows and it never ventured that far away again. However, it did remain curious about what was out there beyond its familiar boundaries. I've loved you, waited for a kind word from you, patiently, humbly. Dunk, Dunk, Dribble, Dribble Emergent Literacy Design Emergent Literacy Design Rationale: This lesson will help children identify /d/, the phoneme represented by D. Students will learn to recognize /d/ in spoken words by learning a meaningful representation (a basketball dribbling) and the letter symbol D, practice finding /d/ in words, and apply phoneme awareness with /d/ in phonetic cue reading by distinguishing rhyming words from beginning letters. This lesson is geared towards an appropriate literacy goal for students at this stage of literacy development. Materials: Primary paper and a pencil. Chart that says "Dan dribbles down then dunks"; Dr. Seuss's ABC (Random House, 1963); words cards with DO, DOG, DUCK, DUNK, DARK, DOWN 1. Say: Our written language is a secret code. The trick is learning what the letters stand for- the mouth moves we make as say words. Today we are going to work on spotting the mouth move for /d/. We spell /d/ with the Letter D. D looks like a half moon, and the /d/ sounds like we are dribbling a basketball. 2. Let's pretend to dribble our basketball. When I say go, I want you to dribble three times for /d/, /d/, /d/. (Pantomime a dribbling motion while saying /d/)/ Notice how the tip of your tongue starts at the top of your gums behind your front teeth and pops down? When we say /d/, we are pushing air through our mouths while we move our tongue. Try it! Yes, very good! 3. Let me show you how to find /d/ in the word dribble. I'm going to stretch out the word in slow motion and listen for my dribble sound. Dddd-rr-ii-bb-ll-e. Again. DDDdddd-rrr-ii-bb-l-e. Did you see how my tongue hits the back of my teeth and came down? I can almost feel that dribble sound when my tongue hits the tops of my gums. 4. Let's try a tongue tickler. "Dan dribbles down then dunks." Can we stretch out that drum beat? Every time we hear it is at the beginning of the words. Like this, "Dddan ddddribbles, etc." Now, let's see if we can pull that dribble sound off of our words. "Dddd-an ddd-ribbles dddd-own then ddd-unks, etc." 5. (Bring out primary paper and pencil) We use the letter D to spell /d/. Captial D looks like a half moon. We can also practice lowercase d. First, make a lowercase c. Then, right next to the c, begin at the rooftop, and connect a straight line on the side for our c until we reach the sidewalk. Once I see that you have it, and I give you a sticker for your hard work, I want you to make nine more just like it! 6. I want you to listen to the words and sounds I am going to say. You can tell me if you hear /d/ in these words. Do you hear /d/ in dream or awake? Down or up? Cat or dog? Now, dribble your basketball if you hear /d/ in any of the words I say. "Time, dear, book, monkey, dessert, double, rose" 7. Say: "Let's look at an alphabet book. Dr. Seuss tells us about a boy having a silly dream whose name starts with a D. Can you guess?" Read the D page drawing out /d/. Ask children if they can think of other words that start with /d/. Make up a silly dream like David Donald Doo's and include pictures. I will display these on the letter board. 8. Show DOG and model how to decide if it is DOG or LOG. The D tells me to dribble my basketball, so this word is ddd-og, dog. You try some: DUNK: dunk or skunk? DOWN: down or frown? DUCK: luck or duck? 9. For assessment, distribute the worksheet. Students are to complete the partial spellings and color the pictures that begin with F. Call students individually to read the phonetic cue words from step #8. Return to the Caravans index. Let's imagine you have a giant basket of apples, and you want to figure out how tall the apple tree must be to grow so many apples. You notice that the more apples you have, the taller the tree should be. However, it's not just about having more apples; some trees naturally produce bigger apples than others, even if both trees have the same number of apples! In our example, let's think of the "order of growth" as how tall the tree is, while each apple corresponds to the values of a special mathematical function called f(z). This function grows larger when z becomes larger, similar to how a tree gets taller as its branches extend further from the base. When the function f(z) has a certain "order of growth," denoted by , it means that f(z) cannot grow faster than the exponential function e^B\*|z|^ , no matter how big z gets. Now, suppose your apple basket originally had some bad apples near the bottom, making counting difficult. To make things easier, you remove those lower-level bad apples-which correspond to removing the first few terms of the function without changing the overall pattern of growth. In other words, you are now considering F(z), which equals f(z) divided by (z^ ell), where ell represents the number of bad apples removed. The question then arises: does F(z) still maintain a reasonable "order of growth"? It turns out that yes, it does! Since multiplying or dividing by powers of z doesn't change the essential nature of the exponential bound on f(z)'s growth, the new function F(z) will also share the same property. Specifically, F(z) won't grow any faster than e^B\*|z|^ , ensuring that it maintains a manageable size, regardless of how large z may become. That's why F(z) retains an order of growth less than or equal to after eliminating the initial hindrances caused by the lowermost bad apples.
